rag.description: The SwitchAlert action switches WebDriver focus to the active browser alert dialog and performs one of three outcomes: accept (alert.Approve()), dismiss (alert.Close()), or no-action pass-through. When invoked with no parameters and no Argument, the plugin dismisses the alert immediately via the early-exit branch (Parameters.Count == 0 AND Rule.Argument is empty). AlertAction is resolved from the named parameter first; if absent, the raw Rule.Argument string is used as the fallback. Only 'ACCEPT' and 'DISMISS' (case-insensitive) trigger the corresponding alert method — any other value is a deliberate no-action pass-through. The optional Keys parameter sends text via alert.SendKeys() only when the value is non-null and non-empty; an empty string bypasses the SendKeys call entirely.

qa.question: What does SwitchAlert do when invoked with no argument and no parameters?
qa.answer: When both pluginData.Parameters.Count is 0 and pluginData.Rule.Argument is empty, the plugin switches to the alert and immediately calls alert.Close() to dismiss it, then returns without executing any further alert logic.

qa.question: How do you accept an alert dialog with SwitchAlert?
qa.answer: Set Argument to 'Accept' or supply the AlertAction named parameter with value 'Accept'. The comparison is case-insensitive (OrdinalIgnoreCase), so 'ACCEPT', 'accept', and 'Accept' all trigger alert.Approve(). Values with leading or trailing whitespace do not match and will fall through to no-action.

qa.question: How do you send text to a prompt dialog and then accept it in a single step?
qa.answer: Use the argument format '{{$ --Keys:your text --AlertAction:Accept}}'. The Keys parameter sends the text via alert.SendKeys() before AlertAction is evaluated and alert.Approve() is called.

qa.question: What happens when AlertAction is 'Ignore' or any unrecognized value?
qa.answer: AlertAction is compared only against 'ACCEPT' and 'DISMISS' (case-insensitive). Any other value falls through both branches — neither alert.Approve() nor alert.Close() is called. Keys are still sent if the Keys value is non-empty. This is the intentional no-action pass-through behavior.

qa.question: What is the AlertAction resolution order?
qa.answer: AlertAction is resolved via pluginData.Parameters.Get('AlertAction', pluginData.Rule.Argument). The named AlertAction parameter takes priority; if absent, the raw Argument string is used as the fallback.

qa.question: What happens when the Keys parameter is an empty string?
qa.answer: The code guards the SendKeys call with !string.IsNullOrEmpty(keys). An empty string causes string.IsNullOrEmpty to return true, so alert.SendKeys() is not called. Providing --Keys: with no value is functionally identical to omitting the Keys parameter entirely.

qa.question: What is the manifest version for SwitchAlert?
What alias is available for SwitchAlert?
qa.answer: SwitchAlert is also accepted as SwitchToAlert — both resolve to identical behavior.

description: ### Purpose
Switches WebDriver focus to the active browser alert dialog and performs a configurable action: accept, dismiss, or no-action pass-through.
An optional `Keys` parameter sends text to prompt dialogs before the action is applied.
When invoked with no argument and no parameters, the alert is dismissed by default.
### Key Features and Functionality
| Feature            | Description                                                                                                                                            |
| Default Dismiss    | Dismisses the alert when invoked with no argument and no parameters (early-exit branch: `Parameters.Count == 0` AND `Argument` is empty).              |
| Accept             | Calls `alert.Approve()` when `AlertAction` resolves to `Accept` (case-insensitive, exact match — whitespace is not trimmed).                           |
| Dismiss            | Calls `alert.Close()` when `AlertAction` resolves to `Dismiss` (case-insensitive, exact match — whitespace is not trimmed).                            |
| Ignore / No-Action | Any `AlertAction` value other than `Accept` or `Dismiss` — including `Ignore` — is a deliberate pass-through; neither accept nor dismiss is performed. |
| Keys Input         | Sends text via `alert.SendKeys()` only when `Keys` is non-null and non-empty; always executed before the accept/dismiss action.                        |
### Usages in RPA
| Usage                       | Description                                                                                                    |
| Alert Notification Handling | Automatically accept or dismiss confirmation dialogs triggered during automated form submission or navigation. |
| Prompt Input Automation     | Send dynamic text to prompt dialogs and accept them to satisfy required inputs without manual intervention.    |
| Error Recovery              | Dismiss unexpected alert dialogs to allow the automation process to recover and continue execution.            |
### Usages in Automation Testing
| Usage                  | Description                                                                             |
| Alert Acceptance Tests | Verify that the application responds correctly when alert dialogs are accepted.         |
| Dismiss Behavior       | Confirm that dismissing an alert leaves the application in the expected state.          |
| Prompt Input Testing   | Validate that text entered in prompt dialogs is processed correctly by the application. |

edge_cases: Both Parameters.Count == 0 and Rule.Argument must be empty simultaneously — providing even one named parameter or a non-empty Argument bypasses the early-exit and enters the main alertAction branch

Rule.Argument is whitespace-only — string.IsNullOrEmpty returns false for whitespace, so the early-exit path is skipped; alertAction becomes the whitespace string, which matches neither ACCEPT nor DISMISS, and no accept or dismiss is called
The active alert was already handled — WebDriver.SwitchTo().Alert() throws NoAlertPresentException if no alert is present when the plugin runs
Multiple alerts queued — only the currently frontmost alert is targeted; each subsequent alert requires a separate SwitchAlert invocation
annotations.expected_result: The active alert dialog is dismissed (alert.Close() is called) because no argument and no parameters are provided.
annotations.notes: This is the early-exit branch in the code: both Parameters.Count == 0 and Rule.Argument being empty must be true simultaneously for this path to execute.

description: ### Dismiss an alert by default (no argument)
Dismiss the active alert dialog without providing any argument or parameters.
When both `Parameters.Count` is 0 and `Argument` is empty, the plugin takes the early-exit dismiss path and calls `alert.Close()` immediately.

edge_cases: Argument has leading or trailing whitespace — ' Accept '.Equals('ACCEPT', OrdinalIgnoreCase) is false; alert.Approve() is NOT called and the alert is neither accepted nor dismissed
Alert is a confirm dialog — alert.Approve() clicks OK, confirming the dialog and allowing the page action to proceed
Alert is a beforeUnload dialog — some browsers restrict programmatic accept on beforeUnload prompts; driver behavior may vary across environments
Alert is already dismissed externally — NoAlertPresentException is thrown by WebDriver.SwitchTo().Alert() before alert.Approve() is reached
annotations.expected_result: The active alert dialog is accepted (alert.Approve() is called).
annotations.notes: The Argument value 'Accept' resolves as the fallback for AlertAction because no named parameters are present. StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase makes 'accept', 'Accept', and 'ACCEPT' equivalent.

description: ### Accept an alert dialog
Accept the active alert dialog by setting `Argument` to `Accept`.
The value is compared case-insensitively, so `ACCEPT`, `accept`, and `Accept` all trigger `alert.Approve()`.

edge_cases: Any non-empty, non-ACCEPT, non-DISMISS AlertAction value produces the same no-action pass-through as 'Ignore' — including typos such as 'Acccept' or unrelated strings
Keys parameter is also supplied alongside Ignore AlertAction — alert.SendKeys() is still called before the pass-through; the alert receives the text input but remains open

Argument resolves to the raw template string when only Keys is provided via '{{$ --Keys:text}}' — AlertAction falls back to that raw string, which matches neither ACCEPT nor DISMISS, producing the same pass-through behavior
Alert is not present — NoAlertPresentException is thrown by WebDriver.SwitchTo().Alert() before the pass-through logic is reached
annotations.expected_result: WebDriver switches to the alert but neither accepts nor dismisses it. The alert remains open.
annotations.notes: The code only branches on 'ACCEPT' and 'DISMISS'. Any other value, including 'Ignore', falls through both branches. This is the intentional no-action pass-through — the alert stays open after this step.

description: ### Ignore an alert (no-action pass-through)
Switch to the alert dialog without accepting or dismissing it by setting `Argument` to `Ignore`.
Because `Ignore` is neither `Accept` nor `Dismiss`, both action branches are skipped and the alert remains open.
Any value other than `Accept` or `Dismiss` produces the same no-action result.

edge_cases: Keys is an empty string — string.IsNullOrEmpty('') is true, so alert.SendKeys() is NOT called; behavior is equivalent to omitting the Keys parameter entirely
Keys is whitespace-only — string.IsNullOrEmpty(' ') is false, so alert.SendKeys(' ') is called with the whitespace value
Alert is not a prompt dialog — calling SendKeys on an alert or confirm type is driver-dependent and may throw or be silently ignored
No AlertAction is specified — alertAction resolves to the raw Argument string, which matches neither ACCEPT nor DISMISS; the alert remains open after keys are sent
annotations.expected_result: The text 'Foo Bar' is sent to the prompt dialog via alert.SendKeys(). No accept or dismiss action is taken; the alert remains open.
annotations.notes: Using Keys without AlertAction intentionally leaves the alert open after the text is entered. Pair with --AlertAction:Accept or --AlertAction:Dismiss to close the dialog in the same step.

description: ### Send keys to a prompt dialog (no action)
Send the text `Foo Bar` to the active prompt dialog without accepting or dismissing it.
Because no `AlertAction` is specified, only `alert.SendKeys()` is called — the alert remains open after this step.
Pair `Keys` with `AlertAction` in the same invocation when the dialog must also be closed.

edge_cases: Keys is an empty string — string.IsNullOrEmpty('') is true, so alert.SendKeys() is skipped; alert.Approve() still fires because AlertAction resolves to 'Accept'
Keys are always evaluated before AlertAction — the code checks the Keys branch first, then the AlertAction branch, regardless of parameter order in the argument string
Alert is a simple alert with no text input field — SendKeys may be silently ignored by the driver, but alert.Approve() still fires and closes the dialog
Prompt dialog has a pre-filled default value — sending Keys appends to or replaces the existing value depending on the driver and browser
annotations.expected_result: The text 'Foo Bar' is sent to the prompt dialog via alert.SendKeys(), then alert.Approve() is called to accept it.
annotations.notes: This is the standard pattern for prompt dialogs: type into the prompt and confirm. The code always evaluates Keys before AlertAction.

description: ### Send keys to a prompt and accept
Send the text `Foo Bar` to the active prompt dialog and then accept it.
`alert.SendKeys()` is called first; `alert.Approve()` is called immediately after.

edge_cases: Keys is an empty string — alert.SendKeys() is skipped, but alert.Close() still fires because AlertAction resolves to 'Dismiss'
Dismissing a prompt discards any text sent via Keys — the application receives no input value from the canceled dialog, which is the expected cancel behavior
Alert is a confirm dialog — alert.Close() clicks Cancel, which is the equivalent of declining the confirmation and preventing the associated page action
beforeUnload dialog with Dismiss — some browsers prevent programmatic dismissal of beforeUnload dialogs; driver behavior may vary
annotations.expected_result: The text 'Foo Bar' is sent to the prompt dialog via alert.SendKeys(), then alert.Close() is called to dismiss it.
annotations.notes: Dismissing after sending keys is useful for testing that the application handles canceled prompt inputs correctly.

description: ### Send keys to a prompt and dismiss
Send the text `Foo Bar` to the active prompt dialog and then dismiss it.
`alert.SendKeys()` is called first; `alert.Close()` is called immediately after.
Use this pattern to verify that the application handles a canceled prompt input correctly.
Specifies the action to perform on the active alert dialog.
Resolved from the named `AlertAction` parameter first; falls back to the raw `Argument` string if the named parameter is absent.
Comparison is case-insensitive: `Accept`, `ACCEPT`, and `accept` are all equivalent.
parameters.name: AlertAction

description: Calls `alert.Approve()`, equivalent to clicking the `OK` or `Accept` button on the dialog.
values.name: Accept

description: Calls `alert.Close()`, equivalent to clicking the `Cancel` or `Dismiss` button on the dialog.
values.name: Dismiss

description: Neither `alert.Approve()` nor `alert.Close()` is called — the alert dialog remains open.
This is the fallthrough behavior: any `AlertAction` value other than `Accept` or `Dismiss` produces the same no-action result.
values.name: Ignore

description: Text to send to the active alert dialog via `alert.SendKeys()` before `AlertAction` is applied.
Intended for prompt dialogs that require text input.
If omitted or empty, no keys are sent.
parameters.name: Keys

description: Specifies the alert action or provides named parameters for the `SwitchAlert` plugin.
Accepts a plain action value (`Accept`, `Dismiss`, `Ignore`) or a parameterized expression such as `{{$ --Keys:text --AlertAction:Accept}}`.
When absent, the plugin uses the default dismiss behavior.
properties.name: Argument

summary: SwitchAlert switches WebDriver focus to the active browser alert dialog and performs a configurable action: accept, dismiss, or no-action pass-through.

When invoked with no argument and no parameters the alert is dismissed by default. Use `AlertAction:Accept` or `AlertAction:Dismiss` to control the outcome explicitly; use `Keys` to send text to prompt dialogs before the action is taken.
In RPA it automates handling of confirmation and prompt dialogs; in automation testing it validates accept, dismiss, and prompt-input behaviors.
